story:
Waves fold over Claire’s feet as the sun narrows her eyes and salt dampens her face. The Pacific doesn’t notice a few tears. It takes more than that to stir an ocean.

On a cold, smoky night, Claire was headed to the Bee Room when a voice, smooth and low, spilled from Donovan’s honky-tonk on US-97 and pulled her inside. Onstage, a weathered man with a guitar and a dark ring inked around his neck—an SCS mark of the “irredeemable”—sang as if he were wringing truth from his bones. A blue-ink wave tattoo curled from his thumb to his forefinger; his eyes found hers as he leaned into a song about desire and the foolish things it made people do. She drifted to the front as if dreaming.

After his set, Claire followed him to the back. They collided—hunger, heat, the hush of risk—then breathless names: she was Claire; he gave none. At a battered booth guarded by empty bottles, he needed three beers before giving her that name. Chris. Claire ordered more with the confidence that comes from high credit, confidence that evaporated whenever he looked at her.

They fenced in words. He warned he didn’t want to fall in love; she begged him not to mean it. He called their meeting a wicked game: someone like her and someone like him shouldn’t be together, even in shadow. She wanted to erase his mark, pull him into the bright world her points could buy. He refused. He’d earned the stigma, he said, and would not deny it. He lit a cigarette, defiant and calm, and told her power’s rules weren’t the only currency—cash still found hands, motels still found rooms, people still acted like people, even in her brave new world.

Chris said he wasn’t a bomber or a gate-stormer; his crime was refusing to chant, refusing to sign, refusing to clap for petty masters. He was not gentle, he said, but his violence had a narrow aim: a storm he would unleash only against those who would crush the people he loved. He called himself the Pacific—calm on the surface, harboring depth, uncontainable. To be together, either he would have to submit or she would have to become a tempest. Neither was true to their nature.

Claire pleaded anyway—she could hide him; no one had to know. He called it another lie. “You’re a drop keeping yourself safe. I’m a tide. Remember me in the wind and rain,” he said. “Beneath still waters lies the storm.”

A habitual informer in a red pantsuit arrived with two DTES officers and an eager smile. Curfew. Demerits. Claire claimed her authority—regional director of the Social Credit System, attempting rehabilitation. The informer prodded: was he employed? Chris snapped, half rising from the booth and angling himself in front of Claire: “Of course not. I can’t be paid. I’m a scavenger she bought a beer for to talk compliance.” The officers took him anyway. As they dragged him off, he smiled at Claire.

“Beneath still waters lies the storm.”

In the months that followed, each Thursday she returned to their booth. Each night she woke from dreams of him, her heart aching for a presence that wasn’t there.

Now, at the coast, she lets the sand swallow her steps. The water laps calm, but far beyond, a dark line draws across the horizon. Tears salt her cheeks.

Claire has played a wicked game. The sea is coming. She waits for the storm.
